questions.text: Which of the following is a 'Public Facility'?
questions.explanation: Public facilities are essential services provided to a large number of people simultaneously.

questions.text: What is the most important characteristic of a public facility?
options: Once it is provided, its benefits can be shared by many people
It is only for the rich
It is provided by private companies only
questions.explanation: Sharing benefits is the core idea (e.g., a school benefits many children in the locality).

questions.text: The 'Right to Water' is a part of the Fundamental Right to ________.
questions.explanation: Courts have recognized that access to safe drinking water is essential for life.

questions.text: Whose primary responsibility is it to provide public facilities?
questions.explanation: Since most public facilities don't make profit, the government must ensure they are available to all.

questions.text: Why are private companies often uninterested in providing public facilities?
options: There is no profit to be made in many of them (like street lighting)
They are banned from doing so
questions.explanation: Private companies operate mainly for profit, whereas public facilities focus on welfare.

questions.text: Where does the government get the money to provide public facilities?
questions.explanation: Taxes on income, goods, and services fund the government's budget for public services.

questions.text: The situation in Chennai (as described in the book) shows that water is ________.
options: Not available equally; the poor suffer the most
questions.explanation: Rich areas get regular supply, while poor colonies and slums face severe shortages.

questions.text: What is 'Sanitation'?
options: Provisions for safe disposal of human urine and faeces
questions.explanation: Sanitation is vital to prevent water-borne diseases like cholera and jaundice.

questions.text: According to the UN, the 'Right to Water' means:
options: Water should be free for everyone
Everyone has a right to sufficient, safe, and affordable water
People can use as much water as they want
Water should be sold in bottles
questions.explanation: The right includes quality, quantity, and accessibility.

questions.text: Which city in the world has achieved a zero-infant mortality rate through excellent public water systems?
questions.explanation: The municipal water department in Porto Alegre is a model of efficient public service.

questions.text: A 'Budget' is an account of:
options: The government's planned expenses and income for the coming year
The money in the PM's office
questions.explanation: The budget presented in Parliament details how the government plans to spend tax money.

questions.text: Shortage of public water often leads to the growth of ________.
options: Private water tankers and bottled water business
questions.explanation: When the government fails, private companies step in and charge high prices.

questions.text: Sulabh International is an NGO working primarily in the field of ________.
questions.explanation: Sulabh has built thousands of public toilets to improve sanitation for the poor.

questions.text: The supply of water per person in a city should ideally be ________ liters per day (Indian standard).
questions.explanation: The standard set by the Urban Water Supply and Sanitation Committees is 135 liters per day.

questions.text: What happens when public facilities are handed over to private companies?
options: Prices often rise, making them unaffordable for the poor
questions.explanation: Private companies focus on profit, which can lead to high costs for essential services.

questions.text: In Bolivia, the protest against the privatisation of ________ led to a 'Water War'.
questions.explanation: Massive protests forced the government to take back control from the private company.

questions.text: Safe drinking water can prevent ________.
questions.explanation: Diseases like diarrhoea and dysentery are mainly caused by contaminated water.

questions.text: Slum areas in cities often have ________ access to public facilities.
questions.explanation: Slums are frequently neglected in the planning and provision of urban infrastructure.

questions.text: Which fundamental right is violated if a city provides water only to its rich residents?
questions.explanation: Discrimination in access to essential services violates the principle of equality.

questions.text: The 'Universal Access' to water means:
options: Only people with cars get water
Every person has enough water for their needs at an affordable price
Water is sent to other planets
questions.explanation: Universal access implies no discrimination based on income or location.

questions.text: Who is responsible for street lights and garbage collection in a city?
questions.explanation: Local urban bodies handle basic civic amenities.

questions.text: A 'Toll' is a fee collected for using which public facility?
questions.explanation: Tolls help recover the cost of construction and maintenance of roads.

questions.text: What is the result of using contaminated water?
questions.explanation: Infants are most vulnerable to water-borne infections.

questions.text: The government's main source of revenue is ________.
questions.explanation: Taxes like GST and Income Tax provide the bulk of the funds.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a public facility?
questions.explanation: Private tuition centers are private commercial ventures, not public facilities.

questions.text: A 'Subsidised' price means:
options: A price lower than the actual cost, with the government paying the difference
questions.explanation: Subsidies make essential goods affordable for everyone.

questions.text: What is the problem with relying on private water tankers?
options: They are not regulated and can be very expensive
questions.explanation: Private tankers often exploit shortages to make high profits.

questions.text: Why do some people say 'Water is a social good, not a commodity'?
options: Because it's a basic need for survival and should not be sold for profit
questions.explanation: This view opposes the privatisation of water resources.

questions.text: Public Transport includes ________.
questions.explanation: Mass transit systems are essential public facilities in cities.

questions.text: Infant Mortality Rate refers to the number of children that die before the age of ________.
questions.explanation: It is a key indicator of the health and sanitation status of a society.

questions.text: In India, the percentage of households with access to safe drinking water is ________ (historically).
questions.explanation: Large sections of the population still lack piped water supply.

questions.text: The Constitution of India recognizes the 'Right to Education' for all children between the ages of ________.
questions.explanation: The Right to Education (RTE) Act makes it a fundamental right.

questions.text: What happens in a 'Water War'?
options: Violent protests against high water prices or privatisation
questions.explanation: The term was popularized by the protests in Bolivia.

questions.text: Pollution-free air is also considered a part of the ________.
questions.explanation: Clean environment is essential for a healthy life.

questions.text: Municipal water supply is often ________ in summer months.
questions.explanation: Scarcity leads to reduced supply hours and frequency.

questions.text: Private schools and hospitals are usually located in ________ areas.
questions.explanation: They follow the market demand where people can pay higher fees.

questions.text: Who pays for the street lights that we use every night?
questions.explanation: It is a classic public facility funded by the state.

questions.text: The most effective way to provide water to all is through ________.
questions.explanation: Piped systems are the most efficient for mass distribution.

questions.text: A 'Common' resource is one that:
options: Is available for use by all members of a community
questions.explanation: Public parks and village wells are common resources.

questions.text: Inland waterways and railways are examples of ________.
questions.explanation: They are shared transportation infrastructures provided for the public.
